title: Weyl Law for the phase transition spectrum and density of limit-interfaces
speaker: Marco Méndez Guaraco
abstract: Abstract: The Allen-Cahn equation behaves as a desingularization of the area functional. This allows for a PDE approach to the construction of minimal hypersurfaces in closed Riemannian manifolds. After presenting and overview of the subject, I will discuss recent results regarding a Weyl Law and its consequences for the density of minimal hypersurfaces in generic metrics. This is joint work with P. Gaspar.
tags: Workshop on Mean Curvature and Regularity

proof_stages.label: Allen–Cahn variational limit-interface setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a Riemannian manifold, the Allen–Cahn equation as the Euler–Lagrange equation of an energy, the Morse index of a critical point, and the zero-level interfaces whose bounded-energy/bounded-index limits are expected to be minimal hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Minimal-hypersurface template and target theorems
proof_stages.summary: The density theorem of Irie–Marques–Neves and the Weyl law for the area functional are recalled as the model, and the Allen–Cahn goal is stated as a Weyl law plus, in dimension three, generic density of separating embedded limit interfaces.

proof_stages.label: Compactness and correspondence inputs
proof_stages.summary: Weak varifold convergence, index semicontinuity, the three-dimensional strong convergence theorem, and the Pacard–Ritoré existence theorem provide the dictionary between bounded Allen–Cahn solutions and minimal hypersurfaces or limit interfaces.

proof_stages.label: Allen–Cahn min-max spectrum
proof_stages.summary: The phase-transition min-max values L_p are produced via the u↦−u symmetry and RP^∞-type cohomological families, with controlled index/nullity and normalization by the one-dimensional transition energy.

proof_stages.label: Weyl law for phase-transition widths
proof_stages.summary: The Weyl law for L_p is stated and its proof is sketched by adapting the area-functional strategy: subadditivity and rescaling for cubes, extension to Euclidean domains, and then manifold decompositions glued with controlled boundary truncations.

proof_stages.label: Open dense reduction for interface density
proof_stages.summary: For each open set U, density is reduced to showing that metrics admitting a separating nondegenerate limit interface meeting U form an open dense set, with openness following from the inverse function theorem and Pacard–Ritoré.

proof_stages.label: Perturbative density contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Density is proved by locally perturbing a bumpy metric to change volume, using the Weyl law to force a change in some L_p, and invoking local bumpy-metric plus strong-convergence input to ensure that the resulting interface is separating and nondegenerate.

examples.name: Standard Allen–Cahn double-well nonlinearity
examples.note: The nonlinearity represented by W'(u)=u^3-u is mentioned only to make the semilinear Allen–Cahn equation concrete, not to supply an idea used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the quartic Allen–Cahn double-well concretely, deriving the radial energy profile as a symmetric degree-four polynomial with a unique candidate critical point and discussing the 1D hyperbolic-tangent profile/energy constant

passes.reason: the canonical double-well nonlinearity is an even/odd reflection-symmetric model used illustratively, not as a failure case
The standard Allen–Cahn quartic double-well is an even, balanced two-well model with the usual u↦−u symmetry, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
the standard quartic double-well is a Z2-symmetric Allen–Cahn potential used as the basic model, not to refute a claim
The standard Allen–Cahn double-well is the usual balanced even potential/odd cubic nonlinearity used as a model, not deployed to refute anything.
The standard quartic Allen–Cahn double-well has the canonical even/odd [math]u\mapsto -u[/math] symmetry with identical wells, and it is used only as the model nonlinearity.
The standard quartic Allen–Cahn double-well is an ordinary model potential, with the usual ±1 symmetry, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
It is used as the standard Allen–Cahn choice, and the even double-well potential/odd nonlinearity has the usual sign symmetry rather than serving as a refutation.
the standard balanced double-well Allen–Cahn nonlinearity is used as the model potential, with its intrinsic u↦−u symmetry but not as a failure case
Used as the standard illustrative Allen–Cahn nonlinearity, with the usual sign symmetry of the equal double wells.
the standard quartic double-well/odd Allen--Cahn nonlinearity is used as the model setting and has the intrinsic ± symmetry of equal wells
The standard Allen–Cahn double-well potential/nonlinearity is used as the model case and has the intrinsic Z2 symmetry between the two wells.
the standard quartic Allen–Cahn double-well is used as the canonical phase-transition model and has the usual sign-exchange symmetry, not as an obstruction

examples.name: Neumann Laplacian min-max analogy
examples.note: The familiar fact that unconstrained variational problems for the Laplacian on a domain produce Neumann boundary conditions is used as an analogy for the Allen–Cahn boundary version in the Weyl-law proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the Neumann Laplacian is invoked as an analogy for what min-max without boundary conditions yields, but no computation or construction is carried out for the Laplacian itself

passes.reason: the Neumann Laplacian is invoked as a standard boundary-condition analogy for the min-max construction, not as a structurally special object or counterexample
The Neumann Laplacian is invoked as an ordinary variational analogy for natural boundary conditions, not as a structural edge case or disproof.
the Neumann Laplacian is an ordinary boundary-value spectral problem invoked only as an analogy for the Allen–Cahn min-max construction
The Neumann Laplacian is used only as a standard variational analogy for natural boundary conditions in min-max, not as an adversarial example.
The Neumann Laplacian variational problem is a standard boundary-value case invoked as an analogy for Allen–Cahn min-max, not to refute any claim.
The Neumann Laplacian is invoked as a standard boundary-condition analogy for the Allen–Cahn min-max problem, not as a structurally special object or counterexample.
It is invoked only as an analogy explaining why boundary min-max gives Neumann conditions, with the Neumann Laplacian treated as a standard typical operator.
the Neumann Laplacian min-max problem is invoked as a standard analogy for the boundary Allen–Cahn construction, not as an obstruction or structurally special object
Used only as an analogy for min-max on a domain yielding natural Neumann boundary conditions, not as a failure case or structurally special operator.
the Neumann Laplacian is invoked as a standard analogy for the natural boundary condition in min-max, not as an obstruction or special case
The Neumann Laplacian min-max situation is invoked as an explanatory analogy for natural boundary conditions, not as an adversarial example, and is structurally an ordinary boundary spectral problem.
the Neumann Laplacian is invoked as an ordinary variational analogy for boundary min-max problems, not to refute a claim
